---
role: proof
locale: en
of_concept: ergodic-decomposition-theorem
source_book: gtm046
source_chapter: "IX"
source_section: "33"
---

**Proof.** Since the Borel $\sigma$-field $\mathcal{B}$ is generated by a denumerable field $\{S(n)\}$ of Borel sets (for instance, the field of finite unions of intervals with rational endpoints) and the conditional probability $\bar{P}(x, S)$ given the $\sigma$-field $\mathcal{C}$ of invariant Borel sets is regularized (by the Regularity Theorem), the decomposition theorem 26.2A applies. This yields the asserted decomposition

$$\bar{P}S = \int_T \bar{P}_t S \, \mu(dt),$$

with invariant atoms $S_t$ such that $\bar{P}(x, S_t) = 1$ for $\bar{P}$-almost every $x \in S_t$, and the properties of $\bar{P}(x, S_t)$ and $\bar{P}(x, S)$ follow.

For the invariance of the $\bar{P}_t$: by the Vanishing Singular Transition Probability Theorem (Theorem A), for every fixed Borel set $S$,

$$\bar{P}(x, S) = \int \bar{P}(x, dy) P(y, S), \quad x \notin N_S, \quad \bar{P} N_S = 0,$$

where $P(y, S)$ is the one-step transition probability. From this identity it follows that

$$\bar{P}_t S = \int \bar{P}_t(dx) P(x, S)$$

holds except for indices $t \in T_S \subset T$ corresponding to sets $S_t$ of total $\bar{P}$-probability zero. Adding such exceptional sets for $S = S(1), S(2), \ldots$ to the $P$-null set $N$ of the partition, the last equality holds for all remaining $t$'s and every $S(n)$. Since the $\{S(n)\}$ form a field generating the Borel $\sigma$-field, the equality extends to all Borel sets $S$, proving the invariance of each $\bar{P}_t$.

For the converse: integrating the invariance relation $\bar{P}_t S = \int \bar{P}_t(dx) P(x, S)$ with respect to the mixing measure $\mu$ yields that any mixture $\bar{P}'S = \int \bar{P}_t S \, \mu(dt)$ of invariant probabilities is itself invariant:

$$\bar{P}' S = \int \bar{P}_t S \, \mu(dt) = \int \left(\int \bar{P}_t(dx) P(x, S)\right) \mu(dt) = \int \bar{P}'(dx) P(x, S).$$

If an invariant probability $\bar{P}'$ is $\bar{P}$-continuous, then by the Radon-Nikodym theorem it can be represented as $\bar{P}'S = \int_S h \, d\bar{P}$ with a nonnegative $\mathcal{C}$-measurable density $h$. Decomposing $h$ according to the partition $\{S_t\}$ yields the representation $\bar{P}'S = \int \bar{P}_t S \, \mu'(dt)$ for some measure $\mu'$ on $T$. This completes the proof.
